name: NaN Boxing
summary: Encode pointers, integers, booleans, and other tagged values inside the unused bit patterns of IEEE 754 NaN doubles. Every value fits in a 64-bit register; doubles store directly without conversion. Used by SpiderMonkey, LuaJIT, and JavaScriptCore.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
NaN boxing exploits the IEEE 754 double-precision format: any value with all 11 exponent bits set and a non-zero mantissa is a NaN (Not a Number). There are 2^52 possible NaN bit patterns, but only one is needed for the canonical NaN. The remaining patterns can encode pointers, integers, booleans, nil, and other tagged values — all in 64 bits.

For dynamically typed languages, every runtime value must carry enough type information to dispatch correctly. NaN boxing fits that information into a single 64-bit machine word: doubles store directly, everything else encodes into the spare NaN payload.

sections.title: Encoding scheme
sections.content:
A 64-bit IEEE 754 double has the bit pattern `s eeeeeeeeeee mmmm…m` (1 sign + 11 exponent + 52 mantissa). A NaN sets all exponent bits and any non-zero mantissa. If the value's exponent bits are not all-set, the value is a real finite double and stored directly. If the exponent bits are all-set and the mantissa matches a chosen reserved NaN pattern, the value is canonical NaN. Otherwise, the high mantissa bits encode a type tag, and the remaining bits encode the payload — typically a 48-bit pointer (sufficient on current x86-64) or a small integer.

The payload-extraction operation is a mask. The fast path for arithmetic is: check whether both operands are real doubles by comparing the exponent against the all-set pattern; if so, do a hardware double-add directly. The slow path is dispatched only when the type check fails.

sections.title: Production usage
sections.content:
SpiderMonkey (Firefox), LuaJIT, and JavaScriptCore use NaN boxing. The advantage: every value fits in a 64-bit register, enabling register-width operations and avoiding heap allocation for numbers. A double is stored directly; everything else is stored in the NaN mantissa bits with a type tag.

The disadvantage: extracting a pointer requires masking off tag bits, and storing a double requires checking for and avoiding the NaN range collisions when the user's code legitimately produces a NaN. Some operations need extra branches. Also, only 48-bit pointers fit in the NaN payload, which is sufficient on current x86-64 hardware but not future-proof for systems that extend the virtual address space.

sections.title: Trade-offs vs alternatives
sections.content:
NaN boxing optimises for the case where most values are floating-point doubles — typical of JavaScript and Lua workloads. Doubles are zero-overhead; integers and pointers pay a small mask. If most values are pointers, tagged-pointer schemes (which steal low bits from aligned pointers) are usually preferable, because they impose no penalty on the dominant case.

The ExBoxing hybrid bridges the two: tag pointers in the low bits for the pointer-heavy case, and reserve a tag value for an extended encoding of common doubles. Most NaN-boxing engines also include a Smi (small-integer) fast path that avoids the floating-point check entirely for integer-arithmetic-heavy code.
